articles.title: What God Said in the Night
articles.dek: Paul survived a mob, a near-flogging, and a council that nearly tore him apart — all in forty-eight hours. That night, God came to his cell with a single sentence.
articles.quote: Take courage! As you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome. — Acts 23:11
articles.body:
You've been there. You walk into the room knowing the verdict is already in. Maybe it was the barangay hearing where every neighbor had already signed the complaint. Maybe it was the office investigation where your supervisor wouldn't meet your eyes. Maybe it was the family gathering where the older relatives had already agreed, and you were the last to know. You sat down. You tried to explain. And somewhere in the middle of your sentence, someone interrupted you. Filipinos call it pakiramdam — that wordless knowing before the first accusation is even spoken. You felt the atmosphere. The temperature had already dropped.

That is almost exactly where Paul found himself at the start of Acts 23. Not in a barangay hall. In the Great Sanhedrin of Jerusalem — the supreme religious court of the Jewish people, seventy-one of the most powerful men in the nation. Paul, still bruised from the riot that nearly killed him the day before, still wearing the chains a Roman commander had clasped on his wrists, stood before them.

A quick rewind: the day before had been catastrophic. Paul had followed the Jerusalem elders' instructions to perform a purification ritual in the Temple — a gesture of goodwill. A group from Asia spotted him in the Temple courts, ignited a riot, dragged him out, and nearly beat him to death before a Roman cohort intervened. The commander had him bound in chains. Paul was allowed to address the crowd from the steps — calmly, clearly — until he said the word Gentiles. Then they erupted: "Rid the earth of him! He's not fit to live!" (Acts 22:22). The commander ordered Paul flogged to force the truth out of him. And it was only as the soldiers were stretching him out for the beating that Paul said quietly to the centurion beside him: "Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who hasn't even been found guilty?" (Acts 22:25). The flogging stopped. The commander went pale. He had chained a Roman citizen without a trial — a serious offense.

So now it was the next morning. The commander called the chief priests and Sanhedrin together. Paul looked straight at them — no apology, no posture of submission — and opened with: "My brothers, I have fulfilled my duty to God in all good conscience to this day." (Acts 23:1). Bold. Either the most courageous opening statement ever delivered before a religious court, or the most reckless. Possibly both.

The high priest Ananias had heard enough. He ordered the men near Paul to strike him on the mouth. Not a judicial punishment — a calculated humiliation, designed to break a man's composure and remind him of where he stood. Paul's response came fast: "God will strike you, you whitewashed wall! You sit there to judge me according to the law, yet you yourself violate the law by commanding that I be struck!" (Acts 23:3). Those nearby rebuked him: "You dare insult God's high priest?" Paul paused. Then, with remarkable grace: "Brothers, I did not realize that he was the high priest; for it is written: 'Do not speak evil about the ruler of your people.'" (Acts 23:4–5). He stepped back. Not because he was wrong — Ananias had broken the law — but because the manner of his response needed to honor something higher than his own vindication.

Then something extraordinary happened. Paul saw what was in the room: Pharisees on one side, Sadducees on the other. Two factions that agreed on almost nothing, and disagreed most sharply on the resurrection of the dead. He called out: "My brothers, I am a Pharisee, descended from Pharisees. I stand on trial because of the hope of the resurrection of the dead." (Acts 23:6). The room erupted. The parties turned on each other. The Pharisees began defending Paul. The dispute grew so violent that the Roman commander feared Paul would be torn apart — by his own judges. Soldiers pushed through the mob and pulled him out by force.

And then it was night.

Paul was back in the Roman barracks. The chains were likely back on. The next morning, more than forty men would take an oath not to eat or drink until they had killed him (Acts 23:12–13). The trial had collapsed into chaos. The crowd still wanted him dead. The commander had no idea what to do with him.

And in that night — that specific, ordinary, quiet night after the worst forty-eight hours of Paul's ministry in Jerusalem — the Lord stood near him.

"Take courage! As you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome." — Acts 23:11

Notice what God didn't say. He didn't evaluate Paul's performance or replay the highlights of a brutal two days. He didn't say nice strategy with the Pharisees or good thinking with the citizenship card. He didn't offer an escape plan or explain how Paul would get out of Jerusalem. He said: you must also testify in Rome. While Paul was still processing the wreckage of the council chamber, God had already written the next chapter. The imprisonment was not a dead end. It was a corridor. The story wasn't ending — it had barely started.

Here is what strikes me most: God spoke in the night. Not in the riot. Not when the crowd was screaming for blood. Not when the Sanhedrin tore itself in two. In the night. In the quiet. After the storm had passed and Paul was alone with his thoughts and whatever chains he still wore. Some of us are in that kind of night right now. The worst of it has passed — or it's still settling — and the silence feels heavier than the noise did. You survived the thing that was supposed to break you. And now you're sitting with it, in the dark, not sure what comes next. That is exactly the posture in which God tends to speak.

"Take courage" is not a feeling. It is a direction. When God says those words to Paul, he isn't asking Paul to manufacture a cheerful disposition after two days of near-death experiences. He is giving Paul a bearing — a point on the map called Rome. The courage comes not from feeling better but from knowing there is more road ahead, and the road goes somewhere real. We often wait for courage to arrive as a feeling before we will move. But God gives Paul a destination, not a mood. The courage is embedded in the direction.

You may not know your Rome. You may not know where the road from this particular worst day is leading. But notice the grammar of God's word to Paul: as you have testified here, so you must also testify there. The same Paul who stood calm before the mob. The same Paul who stopped a flogging with six words. The same Paul who disarmed a murderous council with one sentence. That Paul — shaped by everything that had happened — had to get to Rome. What he had been through had made him into someone whose testimony was needed somewhere else.

Tonight, if it has been the kind of day that makes you wonder whether any of it matters — sit in the quiet for a moment. Paul's worst two days in Jerusalem became the road to one of the most consequential missionary careers in history. The trial that collapsed into chaos became the corridor to Caesar's household. The chains became the passage to Rome.

The night is not the end of the story. Sometimes it is the room where God speaks. Take courage.

articles.title: Ask for Something Larger Than Yourself
articles.dek: Solomon had one chance to ask God for anything. What he chose — and what he didn't — reveals the secret behind every lasting kind of wisdom.
articles.quote: The wisest thing you can do is name your inadequacy honestly — and ask God for the wisdom to serve the people standing in front of you.
articles.body:
Imagine you are the eldest child in a Filipino family. Your tatay passed away last year — the cancer took him quickly, quicker than anyone expected. Your nanay is still there, but grief has aged her, and the energy she once had for making decisions has quietly transferred to you. The siblings are still young enough to need steering: the college-age ones asking questions about the future, the youngest still in high school. The relatives have started assuming you will handle things — the papers, the decisions, the phone calls nobody else wants to make. At the last family gathering, one of your titas said it out loud, in that offhanded Filipino way that somehow makes things final: Ikaw na ang haligi ng tahanan. You are the pillar of the home now.

You are thirty years old. You barely feel like an adult yourself. And somehow you are already the person everyone is counting on.

That feeling — that particular mixture of responsibility and inadequacy — has a name in the Bible. And Solomon, of all people, is the one who gave it to us.

David had just died. Solomon stepped onto the throne of Israel young, untested, and carrying more weight than any person should be asked to carry at once. The kingdom was at its peak of power — but it was also complicated: rivals at court who had backed other claimants to the throne, neighboring nations testing the new king's nerve, a people still learning what it meant to be a united nation under God.

In the very first chapter of 2 Chronicles, we see Solomon do something telling. He gathered the leaders of Israel at Gibeon — the most important worship site in the land, where the tabernacle of Moses still stood — and offered a thousand burnt offerings. It was the right move: public, worshipful, showing up where it mattered. The Chronicler notes that God was pleased. And that night, God appeared to the young king with an extraordinary offer.

"That night God appeared to Solomon and said to him, 'Ask for whatever you want me to give you.'" (2 Chr 1:7, NIV)

Read that slowly. The Creator of the universe has just handed a young king a blank check. No conditions. No ceiling. Ask for anything.

What would you have asked for?

Think about what Solomon could have chosen. There were enemies who had opposed his rise to power — he could have asked for their defeat. He was young; he could have asked for a long life. The kingdom was wealthy but always vulnerable; he could have asked for military strength or guaranteed prosperity. Every one of these would have been understandable. None of them would have been wrong.

But here is what Solomon actually said. Not about himself. Not for himself. Read his words carefully:

"Give me wisdom and knowledge, that I may lead this people, for who is able to govern this great people of yours?" (2 Chr 1:10, NIV)

Notice the shape of that request. Solomon does not ask for wisdom in the abstract — not a generic upgrade to his thinking, not a sharpened mind for his own benefit. He asks for wisdom for — that he may lead this people. The request is relational at its core. It is aimed directly at the people he is responsible for.

And then, near the end of his prayer, Solomon does something that is easy to miss: he calls them God's people. Not his subjects. Not his kingdom. Your people, God. As in: they belong to You. I am the one You placed over them, but they are Yours.

That single possessive — yours — is the whole posture of the prayer. Solomon, standing at the peak of earthly power, has already grasped something that takes most leaders a lifetime to learn: the people you lead are not yours to use. They are God's people, entrusted to your care.

This changes everything about how you lead. When the people in your hands belong to God — not to you — you stop managing them for your own benefit and start asking what God wants done for them. You stop asking what will make you look strong and start asking what they actually need.

God's response reveals what He had been watching for all along: "Since this is your heart's desire and you have not asked for wealth, riches or honor, nor for the death of your enemies, and since you have not asked for a long life but for wisdom and knowledge to govern my people over whom I have made you king, therefore wisdom and knowledge will be given you. And I will also give you wealth, riches and honor, such as no king who was before you ever had and none after you will have." (2 Chr 1:11–12, NIV)

God noticed what Solomon didn't ask for. The list of unasked things — riches, honor, revenge, long life — tells its own story. When your deepest desire is to serve the people God has given you rather than to use them, you step into a kind of abundance that self-seeking never finds.

You may not be a king. But you are almost certainly responsible for someone.

A child. A team at work. A youth group. An aging parent who needs decisions made. A younger sibling still finding their way. Maybe you are the one everyone calls when something goes wrong — the de facto family head, the barangay official, the sitio leader, the one who said yes to something and realized too late how large it actually was.

The wisest thing you can do is name your inadequacy honestly — and ask God for the wisdom to serve the people standing in front of you.

Not wisdom in the abstract. Not a more capable version of yourself. Wisdom for them. For these specific people. For this family, this team, this community. Not what would make you look competent — but what they genuinely need from you right now.

That is Solomon's prayer. It is short, honest, and entirely other-directed. You can pray it today.

In chapter 2, we watch Solomon put this wisdom to work almost immediately. He begins organizing the construction of the Temple — a project of staggering scale, requiring tens of thousands of workers, diplomatic negotiations with foreign kings, and craftsmen he simply did not have in his own kingdom. So he writes to Huram king of Tyre, openly asking for skilled workers and materials, acknowledging without embarrassment what he lacks. Even the wisest man in history knew when to ask for help.

Wisdom, it turns out, is not the absence of need. It is the honest acknowledgment of need, paired with the willingness to go to the right source.

There is a version of today where you feel the weight of something you didn't fully choose. The people depending on you. The decisions you didn't ask to make. The quiet fear that you are not quite enough for what has landed in your hands.

Bring that to God. Name the people. Ask not to be relieved of the weight — but for the wisdom to carry it well, for their sake. And watch what He adds when your heart is set not on your own advancement, but on the people He has placed in your care.

articles.title: What We Forget When the Storm Passes
articles.dek: Israel watched the Nile run red, saw frogs overtake a kingdom, and walked out of Egypt in the night — and still forgot. Psalm 78 holds up a mirror that is uncomfortably familiar.
articles.quote: The difference between the people who walk well with God and those who don't often isn't theology. It's memory.
articles.body:
There is a prayer that Filipinos know by heart — not the formal kind, not the Our Father, though most Filipinos know that one too. This is the other prayer. The one whispered in the dark when the storm comes. When the wind is shaking the windows and the rain sounds like gravel thrown at the roof, that's when you hear it: Lord, please. Just get us through this. I'll never forget what you did.

You've probably said a version of it. During a typhoon. During a hospital vigil. During the week you weren't sure how rent would come. You reached for God with both hands — and He was there. You came through. And for a few days afterward, you meant every word. The memory of what He did sat warm in your chest like something precious.

Then three months passed. Then six. And slowly, without you noticing, the memory thinned. The prayer that used to rise from somewhere urgent started coming from somewhere more routine. And somewhere in the distance, an old complaint stirred. God, why is this so hard? Why doesn't anything change?

If that trajectory feels familiar, you are not alone. You are, in fact, in very ancient company.

Psalm 78 is one of the longest psalms — and one of the most honest. It is a poem of national memory, a spiritual audit that the psalmist Asaph conducts on Israel's history. And the audit is not flattering. By the time we reach verses 40 through 48, Asaph has been tracing Israel's cycle for dozens of verses: God does something miraculous, Israel trusts Him briefly, then forgets, then rebels, then grieves Him, then God responds again. And again. And again.

In these verses, Asaph turns the camera toward Egypt — toward the plagues. He lists them not as ancient trivia but as living proof of what Israel had witnessed with their own eyes. The Nile turned to blood. Flies. Frogs. Crops devoured by grasshoppers, then locusts. Vineyards shattered by hail. Livestock struck by lightning. These were not miracles whispered about from generations past. These were things the ancestors themselves had watched happen — signs and wonders in the region of Zoan (Psalm 78:43, NIV), that ancient Egyptian city where Pharaoh held his court.

Israel had front-row seats. They watched the most powerful empire in the known world brought to its knees by the God of a slave nation. And then they were led out — through the sea, across the wilderness — by that same God.

And yet.

They did not remember his power — the day he redeemed them from the oppressor. — Psalm 78:42 (NIV)

Not: they forgot the miracles. Not: they forgot the details. They forgot His power. The force behind the events. The Person behind the signs. They remembered the facts of Egypt as ancient history even as they lived inside a story that hadn't finished yet.

Notice something about the order here. Asaph mentions the forgetting in verse 42, then in verses 43 through 48 he lists the plagues. He is not giving background information. He is presenting evidence. He is asking the reader — he is asking us — to feel the weight of the contradiction. They saw the river run red. They walked out of Egypt in the middle of the night with God parting the sea ahead of them. And they still could not trust that God was present and powerful in the wilderness.

It seems impossible. It seems like something that could only happen to other people, to ancient people, to people who didn't know better. But here is the quiet, uncomfortable truth that Psalm 78 holds up like a mirror: memory is not automatic. Gratitude is not a feeling that sustains itself. The human heart — even a heart that has genuinely experienced God's power — drifts. It drifts toward complaint. It drifts toward forgetfulness. It drifts toward asking, again, as if for the first time: Is God really here? Does He really care?

This is not a character flaw unique to ancient Israel. It is the baseline condition of human beings in every generation. It is you after the typhoon. It is me after the answered prayer. It is anyone who has ever been rescued by grace and found themselves, six months later, starting to wonder if grace is still available.

The difference between the people who walk well with God and those who don't often isn't theology. It's memory. It is the deliberate, practiced act of returning to what God has done and letting it speak into the present moment.

The Hebrew word behind remember in verse 42 — zakar — carries more weight than our English word usually does. To zakar is not just to recall a fact. It is to let that fact shape your present action. When the Old Testament says God remembered His covenant with Abraham, it doesn't mean He had forgotten and suddenly recalled it. It means He acted on it. Memory, in the biblical world, is relational and active. It moves.

So when Israel failed to remember God's power, the failure wasn't a lapse in mental recall. It was a failure to let what they knew inform what they trusted. They knew about the Nile turning to blood. They just couldn't connect that power to the dry and difficult moment they were living in. That is the gap this passage is naming — and the gap this passage is inviting us to close.

It might look like keeping a short list somewhere — not sentimental, not for show, just a record. What was I afraid of? What did God do? How did it end? Read it when the next crisis starts whispering that God is absent or unconcerned. It might look like the habit, before a prayer request, of saying: Lord, I remember what you did in — and filling in the blank. Not as a formula, but as a way of bringing the past into the present, letting the Nile-turning-to-blood God be present in this ordinary, difficult Thursday.

It might simply look like a quiet moment before the notifications arrive — before the news piles on, before the day accelerates — where you sit with what is true: that the God who brought plagues on Egypt and led slaves through the sea has not changed in capability or in commitment.

Psalm 78 is not a shaming psalm. It is a teaching psalm. Asaph tells Israel's story not to condemn them but to break the pattern — to say, in effect: here is what happens when we forget, so let us choose differently. The psalm ends in hope, not despair. It ends with God as shepherd, still tending His flock (Psalm 78:70–72).

You have a history with God. Even if it feels modest. Even if you cannot point to a plague-in-Egypt moment, you can probably point to a time when something was hard and then it wasn't, when something was lost and then it was found, when you prayed and something shifted. That is the history worth keeping.

Don't let it thin out. Don't let the gratitude that was real in the storm become an old story you tell less and less often. Let it be the living thing it was meant to be — the ground you stand on when the next hard thing arrives.

The God who was powerful then has not become ordinary now. Remember His power. Let it hold.

articles.title: The Guide With the Crooked Path
articles.dek: Proverbs 2 doesn't just warn you away from bad choices — it warns you away from the people who have learned to love them. There's a difference. And wisdom helps you see it.
articles.quote: Wisdom doesn't just keep you from wrong choices — it keeps you from the company of people who have learned to love them.
articles.body:
You have done it before. Most Filipinos have. You are running late — maybe it is a delivery, maybe it is a job interview in Makati, maybe it is your ninong's birthday lunch somewhere deep in the maze of Quiapo — and someone offers to show you a faster way. A neighbor, a relative of a relative, someone your tita vouches for. Alam ko 'yung shortcut. You trust them because you trust the person who introduced them. You follow.

And somewhere in the middle of streets you do not recognize, past alleys that feel narrower and darker than they should, you realize something has gone wrong. This person is not bringing you where you need to go. They knew all along where they were taking you. And it was not somewhere good.

That image — the confident guide, the crooked route, the destination that was never what was promised — is exactly what Solomon is warning his son about in Proverbs 2. For the first eleven verses he has been building the case for wisdom: ask for it, dig for it like silver, seek it like hidden treasure (Proverbs 2:4). God will give it freely to those who search. And among wisdom's gifts — one that tends to get overlooked — is this one: it saves you from a very specific kind of danger.

Not just from bad decisions. Not just from moral failures you stumble into alone. From people.

"Wisdom will save you from the ways of wicked men, from men whose words are perverse, who have left the straight paths to walk in dark ways." — Proverbs 2:12–13 (NIV)

Already the description is vivid. These men have left the straight paths. They did not wander off by accident. They departed — deliberately, willfully. They chose to leave the highway for the back alleys. And now they walk in dark ways, which in the Hebrew imagination is not just morally murky — it is places without the light of God's truth. Places where your eyes slowly adjust, and you stop noticing how dark it has gotten, because the darkness has become your normal.

But it is what comes next that should stop you cold.

"...who delight in doing wrong and rejoice in the perverseness of evil, whose paths are crooked and who are devious in their ways." — Proverbs 2:14–15 (NIV)

Notice that word: delight.

Most of us, when we picture people who do wrong, picture reluctance. The one who cheats on their taxes and hates themselves for it. The employee who pads the expense report and feels the guilt gnaw at them overnight. The friend who gossips over lunch and says "I know, I know, I shouldn't, but —" There is still a conscience present. Struggling, maybe losing — but awake.

Verse 14 is describing someone else entirely.

These are people for whom wrong has become pleasure. They are not fighting temptation anymore. They have stopped. And they won — not in the way we hope to win, but in the terrible way where the battle ends because you have crossed to the other side. Delight is a celebration word. Rejoice is the word for a fiesta, for a homecoming, for a wedding. It belongs to joy. And Solomon uses it here — deliberately, chillingly — to describe what these people feel when they do evil. Not shame. Not guilt. Joy.

The moral compass has not just shifted. It has inverted. What should produce shame now produces satisfaction. What should horrify them, thrills them. They are enthusiastic. And that is what makes them so dangerous.

It is worth asking: how does a person get there? Solomon does not fully explain it here, but the rest of Scripture fills in the picture. It starts small. Each compromise makes the next one easier. You silence the conscience enough times and it grows quieter. You rationalize long enough, and one day the rationalization stops being necessary — you simply stop seeing the thing as wrong. What began as struggle became habit. What became habit became identity. What became identity became delight.

And here is the part that matters most for your life today: people who delight in wrong tend to share it. A person ashamed of their choices hides them. A person who rejoices in their choices wants a companion. They have crooked paths — and they are eager to show you the shortcut.

In the Philippines, we are a people who move together. Barkada. Pamilya. Tropa. Our samahan — our togetherness — is one of our greatest gifts and, if we are not careful, one of our greatest vulnerabilities. Because we trust the people around us. And because we trust them, we follow them. We follow where they go on weekends. We follow what they laugh at. We follow the business opportunities they bring us and the invitations they extend. The question Proverbs 2 presses you to ask is not only Am I making good choices? The deeper question is: Who is guiding my feet? And where are their paths going?

The gospel-shaped reading of this promise matters here. It does not mean wisdom turns you into someone cold and suspicious who trusts no one. It means that when you are rooted in God's Word — when you spend real time in it, when your conscience is consistently tuned to it — you begin to notice things. A quiet discomfort that rises before you can articulate why. A sense that something is off about an offer, an invitation, a friendship that seems to want you somewhere you cannot quite name. That is not anxiety. That is not overthinking. That is wisdom doing exactly what Proverbs 2 says it does: saving you from the people whose paths are crooked and whose direction is darkness.

Wisdom doesn't just keep you from wrong choices — it keeps you from the company of people who have learned to love them.

If you are reading this today feeling like you are in an alley you do not recognize — if somewhere in the last season of your life you followed someone who seemed trustworthy and you are now unsettled about where you have ended up — this passage is for you too. The God who says wisdom will save you is not only a God of prevention. He is a God of rescue. Jesus himself warned about wolves in sheep's clothing (Matthew 7:15), and yet the same Jesus is the Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one who has gone the wrong way. He walked into the darkest places to bring people back. He still does.

Turn around. Name the alley. Ask God for the grace to see the way back — and the courage to take it.

For those of you reading this before any wrong turn has happened: stay rooted. Not paranoid. Not closed off. But grounded deeply enough in what the straight path looks like that when a shortcut appears, something in you registers it — before you have even followed. That early warning is wisdom. God gives it freely to all who ask.

Solomon gave this counsel to his son. The Spirit gives it to us today. Do not leave it untouched.
